summary:
The Sukhothai Kingdom (c. 1238–1438 AD) is celebrated in Thai national history as the birthplace of Thai civilization — the first independent Thai-speaking state, established when Thai (Tai) leaders Sri Indraditya and his son broke free from Khmer suzerainty in central Thailand and created a kingdom centered on the city of Sukhothai ('Dawn of Happiness'). The kingdom's most celebrated ruler, Ramkhamhaeng the Great (reigned c. 1279–1298), expanded the kingdom across much of mainland Southeast Asia and is credited with creating the Thai alphabet — a script adapted from Khmer that became the foundation of modern Thai writing.

The Ramkhamhaeng Inscription (c. 1292) — a stone stele attributed to Ramkhamhaeng describing his kingdom's prosperous, just, and open society — is the earliest substantial example of Thai-language writing and one of the most important historical documents in Southeast Asian history, though some scholars have questioned its authenticity. Sukhothai's Buddhism was Theravada, imported from Sri Lanka, and became the dominant form of Buddhism in mainland Southeast Asia (Thailand, Laos, Cambodia, Burma), contrasting with the Mahayana Buddhism of China and Vietnam. Sukhothai artistic tradition — particularly the distinctive Buddha images with their fluid, elongated forms and 'walking Buddha' iconography — defined Thai Buddhist art and continues to influence Thai cultural identity. Sukhothai was gradually absorbed by the more powerful [[event:2309|Ayutthaya Kingdom]] to the south in 1438.
